response_raw:
{
    "mode": "human",
    "topic": "How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.",
    "opinion": "Alright, let me start by saying that I've got a pretty straightforward perspective on this. As someone who's been involved in some of these conversations—mostly because my wife works in public health—I find it frustrating when people bring up things like COVID vaccines without having all the facts straight.

First off, you’ve got to be careful not to jump into any debate right away. I mean, I’m a creative guy who loves messing with pipes and fixing stuff, but even that requires thinking things through before acting. So yeah, approach these discussions calmly; don’t let your emotions get the better of you.

Then there’s the part about knowing what you know. My education is just upper secondary, so not everyone out there has a medical degree like some folks do. But I’ve learned that it pays off to understand basic principles—like how vaccines work in general terms, even if they’re complicated. It helps when people are questioning things.

Also, being somewhat introverted or at least preferring quieter times (my agreeableness score isn’t great) makes it easier for me to listen more than talk in these situations.",
    "reasoning_summary": "To manage discussions about COVID vaccines effectively, one must first remain calm and avoid emotional reactions. Next, gather accurate information from reliable sources before engaging in debates.

Understanding the basics of how vaccines work is crucial even without deep medical knowledge.

Furthermore, being empathetic towards others' concerns while maintaining factual accuracy can help de-escalate tense conversations.",
    "tone": "Calm and grounded despite high neuroticism",
    "citations": ["General knowledge from public health discussions with wife"],
    "used_persona_fields":["age","gender","occupation","psychological_feature"]
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.
response_parsed.opinion:
Alright, let me start by saying that I've got a pretty straightforward perspective on this. As someone who's been involved in some of these conversations—mostly because my wife works in public health—I find it frustrating when people bring up things like COVID vaccines without having all the facts straight.

First off, you’ve got to be careful not to jump into any debate right away. I mean, I’m a creative guy who loves messing with pipes and fixing stuff, but even that requires thinking things through before acting. So yeah, approach these discussions calmly; don’t let your emotions get the better of you.

Then there’s the part about knowing what you know. My education is just upper secondary, so not everyone out there has a medical degree like some folks do. But I’ve learned that it pays off to understand basic principles—like how vaccines work in general terms, even if they’re complicated. It helps when people are questioning things.

Also, being somewhat introverted or at least preferring quieter times (my agreeableness score isn’t great) makes it easier for me to listen more than talk in these situations.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
To manage discussions about COVID vaccines effectively, one must first remain calm and avoid emotional reactions. Next, gather accurate information from reliable sources before engaging in debates.

Understanding the basics of how vaccines work is crucial even without deep medical knowledge.

Furthermore, being empathetic towards others' concerns while maintaining factual accuracy can help de-escalate tense conversations.
response_parsed.tone: Calm and grounded despite high neuroticism
citations: General knowledge from public health discussions with wife
